methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $300M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 1 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Pasqal is a Series B quantum computing company that has raised $300M+ to date. The quantum computing sector comprises 11 total comparables: 3 publicly listed (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum) and 8 private peers including Q-CTRL, Quantinuum, and others at varying stages.

The analysis relies on a single applicable valuation method due to data constraints. Stage-progression modeling, anchored to the $300M total raised as a proxy for implied valuation, applies a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical of Series B advancement to the next institutional round, yielding $570M. A peer-median approach was not feasible: while 7 same-sector comparables exist, only one operates at the same stage and one at an adjacent stage—insufficient for a reliable narrow-band median. Broadening to all-sector peers would risk conflating companies across disparate maturity and market positions, so this method was excluded.

The triangulated valuation range of $484M (low) to $656M (high), with a median of $570M, reflects conservative bounds around the single applicable method rather than true methodological triangulation. Key uncertainties include the representativeness of the 1.9x multiplier for Pasqal's specific risk profile and market conditions, the absence of direct peer revenue or technology-readiness comparables at Series B stage, and structural differences among quantum approaches (photonic versus ion-trap versus superconducting) that may warrant differential valuations.
